contact.title: Contact us
contact.note: This form is a request, not a reservation. We will contact you as soon as possible.

rules.generalText: All accommodations are small in scale: it is easiest to move through the property with hand luggage. We ask that you wear slippers or go barefoot inside; the properties are always beautifully finished. Bed and bath linen are provided. The property is not rented by the day and is not hotel accommodation. We rent for weekends, midweek stays, short stays or longer periods.
items: No parties or celebrations; the property is likewise not rented as a rendezvous location.
Waste must be removed after your stay as agreed

Everything is neatly and beautifully decorated. The property is very child-friendly in terms of views, but the finish is such that we prefer only well-behaved children. The tenant is responsible for any damage and accidents.
We love animals, but not inside the property.

story: In the picturesque, traffic-free Ankerstraatje (= a kind of medieval alley running across the main streets) there are many small fishermen's cottages. (84 m²)

A void above the ground floor at the front façade allows plenty of sunlight to enter through the small windows and offers a remarkable view of city icons such as the Belfry. In the small urban courtyard, an annex was demolished to make way for quality outdoor space. The existing wall creates a pleasant microclimate: sheltered from the wind and, once sprayed with water, cooling on warm summer days.
tips: The promenade along the shipping channel
The fish shops on the Kaai
The Friday morning market in Nieuwpoort town
Restaurants: De wasserette, M Bistro (high end), Takai Bar, Sailors (popular), Brasserie Nieuwpoort.
Bakers: Snauwaert (floor pistolets), Tartelique (fine pastries)
Café The Newport times (local beers)
Beautiful cycling routes
Westfront war museum: view from atop the Albert I monument.
Dronkenput in Middelkerke

story: This home was part of a former urban farm from Nieuwpoort's post-war reconstruction of 1922. Originally it served as the logistical space of the urban farm. It was transformed into a small dwelling. (78.5 m²) The existing pulley system was preserved, as were the in-situ cast floors. Inside, the home was finished exclusively with one type of wood (walnut). The remarkable interior joinery was made by Japan-trained carpenter Mathijs Huyghebaert and artist Frits Jeuris.
The interior is consistently themed. The home has a small sunny courtyard with an outdoor toilet, of which the original WC door with the small heart motif has been preserved.
tips: The promenade along the shipping channel
The fish shops on the Kaai
The Friday morning market in Nieuwpoort town
Restaurants: De wasserette, M Bistro (high end), Takai Bar, Sailors (popular), Brasserie Nieuwpoort.
Bakers: Snauwaert (floor pistolets), Tartelique (fine pastries)
Café The Newport times (local beers)
Beautiful cycling routes
Westfront war museum: view from atop the Albert I monument.
Dronkenput in Middelkerke

story: An uninhabitable house (134 m²) was lovingly restored. The lilies in the fence, the rounded contours and the 'water' theme that inspired it (hence the name "maison d'O") formed the inspiration. Through the many roof windows flanked by mirrors, daylight seeps in via large skylights on the attic level. With the blue ambient light in the attic and the light beams illuminating the rooms below, you feel as if you are staying in a submarine.
tips: The promenade along the shipping channel
The fish shops on the Kaai
The Friday morning market in Nieuwpoort town
Restaurants: De wasserette, M Bistro (high end), Takai Bar, Sailors (popular), Brasserie Nieuwpoort.
Bakers: Snauwaert (floor pistolets), Tartelique (fine pastries)
Café The Newport times (local beers)
Beautiful cycling routes
Westfront war museum: view from atop the Albert I monument.
Dronkenput in Middelkerke

story: This home is part of the social garden district "Eeuwfeestwijk" from 1925. (87 m²) The district was built as a tribute to 100 years of Belgium and remains to this day a close-knit neighbourhood with strong social cohesion. The ground-floor layout is defined by a kitchen kiosk. The front and rear spaces can be separated by closing the shutters. The resident is a chef who passionately and lovingly enjoys experimental cooking for friends. The space and location of the 'kiosk' are fully designed around that. On the upper floors there are two bedrooms, each with its own bathroom. The home has a small sunny courtyard. The remarkable interior joinery was made by Japan-trained carpenter Mathijs Huyghebaert and artist Frits Jeuris.
tips: Shopping stroll in Hasselt city centre
Z 33, beautiful venue
Restaurants: Nostalgia Griek, Taste of India, pocomatto (pasta), favourite Entrepot del Tartufo, and many more...
Cycling through the water (Bokrijk), cycling route network.
